content.text: What the Markov Assumption Says

Any autoregressive model conditions each token on its full history. The Markov assumption is a simplification: the future is conditionally independent of the distant past given the recent k tokens. A kth-order Markov model for sequences writes:

attrs.latex: P(w_t \mid w_1, \ldots, w_{t-1}) = P(w_t \mid w_{t-k}, \ldots, w_{t-1})

content.text: This is the same approximation that underlies N-gram language models (where k = n−1), hidden Markov models (order 1 over the hidden states), and many time-series models. The assumption is never exactly true in natural language, but it becomes accurate enough to be practically useful when combined with enough data and appropriate smoothing.
Why the Assumption Is Necessary

Without any independence assumption, modelling P(wₜ | w₁:t₋₁) exactly requires a separate parameter for every possible history. For a vocabulary of size V and history length t−1, this is V^t configurations — exponential in the sequence length. Even for V=1000 and t=5, that is 10¹⁵ parameters. The kth-order Markov assumption reduces this to V^(k+1) — the number of possible k-grams followed by one more token.
Markov Chains
A first-order Markov chain is the simplest model: the next state depends only on the current state. The full joint probability factorises as:

attrs.latex: P(w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_T) = P(w_1) \prod_{t=2}^{T} P(w_t \mid w_{t-1})

content.text: For a discrete state space of size N, the model is fully specified by an initial distribution π (N-vector) and a transition matrix A (N×N, row-stochastic). Given training data, both are estimated by counting:

import numpy as np
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

def fit_markov_chain(sequences, vocab):
    """
    sequences: list of lists of tokens
    vocab: list of all possible tokens
    Returns: pi (N,), A (N,N)
    """
    idx = {w: i for i, w in enumerate(vocab)}
    N = len(vocab)
    init_counts   = Counter()
    trans_counts  = defaultdict(Counter)

    for seq in sequences:
        if not seq:
            continue
        init_counts[seq[0]] += 1
        for a, b in zip(seq, seq[1:]):
            trans_counts[a][b] += 1

    # Initial distribution
    total = sum(init_counts.values())
    pi = np.array([init_counts.get(w, 0) / total for w in vocab])

    # Transition matrix (add-1 smoothed)
    A = np.zeros((N, N))
    for i, w in enumerate(vocab):
        row_total = sum(trans_counts[w].values()) + N   # Laplace k=1
        for j, v in enumerate(vocab):
            A[i, j] = (trans_counts[w].get(v, 0) + 1) / row_total

    return pi, A

seqs = [
    ["sunny", "sunny", "rainy", "rainy", "sunny"],
    ["rainy", "sunny", "sunny", "sunny"],
    ["sunny", "rainy", "rainy"],
]
vocab = ["sunny", "rainy"]
pi, A = fit_markov_chain(seqs, vocab)
print("pi:", pi.round(3))
print("A:", A.round(3))
Higher-Order Markov Models
A kth-order Markov model treats the last k tokens as a single compound state. For language modelling, k=2 (trigram) is common:

Increasing k captures longer-range dependencies but inflates the number of states (and thus parameters) as N^k. For most NLP use cases, k > 5 is impractical with discrete representations; neural models avoid this by parametrising the context with a continuous embedding rather than enumerating all possible histories explicitly.
Markov Assumption in Time Series
The same principle applies to continuous-valued time series. An AR(p) (autoregressive of order p) model assumes the current value depends linearly on the previous p values plus Gaussian noise:

import numpy as np

def fit_ar(x, p):
    """Fit AR(p) via OLS. x: 1-D array of observations."""
    T = len(x)
    # Design matrix: rows are (x[t-1], x[t-2], ..., x[t-p])
    X = np.column_stack([x[p-i-1:T-i-1] for i in range(p)])
    y = x[p:]
    # OLS: φ = (XᵀX)⁻¹ Xᵀy
    phi, residuals, _, _ = np.linalg.lstsq(X, y, rcond=None)
    return phi

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# Simulate AR(2): x_t = 0.6 x_{t-1} + 0.2 x_{t-2} + noise
phi_true = np.array([0.6, 0.2])
T = 500
x = np.zeros(T)
for t in range(2, T):
    x[t] = phi_true @ x[t-2:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, 0.5)

phi_hat = fit_ar(x, p=2)
print(f"True:      φ = {phi_true}")
print(f"Estimated: φ = {phi_hat.round(3)}")
# True:      φ = [0.6 0.2]
# Estimated: φ = [0.597 0.196]
Stationary Distribution and Mixing
A first-order Markov chain over a finite state space converges to a unique stationary distribution μ when it is ergodic (irreducible and aperiodic). The stationary distribution satisfies:

This is the left eigenvector of A corresponding to eigenvalue 1. The mixing time — how quickly the chain forgets its starting state — is governed by the second-largest eigenvalue of A. Chains with eigenvalues close to 1 mix slowly; chains with eigenvalues close to 0 mix quickly.

# Stationary distribution of the weather chain
A_weather = np.array([[0.7, 0.3],
                      [0.4, 0.6]])
eigenvalues, eigvecs = np.linalg.eig(A_weather.T)
idx = np.argmin(np.abs(eigenvalues - 1.0))
mu = eigvecs[:, idx].real
mu /= mu.sum()
print(f"Stationary: Sunny={mu[0]:.3f}, Rainy={mu[1]:.3f}")
# Stationary: Sunny=0.571, Rainy=0.429

# Second eigenvalue (≈ mixing speed)
lambda2 = sorted(np.abs(eigenvalues))[-2]
print(f"Second eigenvalue: {lambda2:.3f}")   # ~0.3 → fast mixing
Where the Assumption Breaks Down

Long-range dependencies violate finite-order Markov assumptions. In natural language, pronoun coreference ("The scientist … she"), topic coherence across paragraphs, and syntactic agreement across clauses all require information from far beyond the immediate context window. Time series may exhibit seasonality or structural breaks that small-order AR models cannot capture. Neural autoregressive models — RNNs and Transformers — represent the entire history implicitly and do not hard-commit to a fixed Markov order, bypassing this limitation.
